does: This paper introduces RAPL, an observation-only method for learning visual reward functions from minimal human preference feedback to align pre-trained visuomotor robot policies with user preferences. Rather than learning reward models directly from scratch via traditional RLHF, RAPL leverages pre-trained vision encoders and focuses human feedback on fine-tuning these encoders to align with user-preferred visual representations. It then constructs dense visual rewards through feature matching in the aligned representation space, significantly reducing the amount of human feedback required compared to standard reinforcement learning from human feedback approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research applying RLHF to embodied agents and extends recent successes with pre-trained foundation models in robotics. It responds to the fundamental challenge that RLHF has proven prohibitively sample-inefficient for learning visual reward functions in visuomotor control, unlike its effectiveness in language models. The approach combines insights from representation learning and preference-based learning, drawing on prior work showing that human feedback is more efficiently used to align representations than to directly specify rewards, while leveraging the benefits of large-scale pre-trained vision encoders that have demonstrated strong transfer properties in robotics.
enables: This work enables more practical deployment of pre-trained visuomotor policies by reducing the human feedback burden for policy alignment, moving toward real-world robotics applications where collecting extensive preference annotations is infeasible. By demonstrating that alignment can occur primarily through representation fine-tuning rather than extensive reward model training, the approach opens avenues for combining foundation models with lightweight human feedback mechanisms. Future work could extend this to multi-task alignment, investigate how representation alignment transfers across different policy architectures, explore active learning strategies to further minimize feedback, and apply the method to real robot platforms where preference gathering is costly.
